findings.rationale: PR replaces ~1200-line prompt chain with deterministic Python CLI control plane. 10 components affected (7 new, 3 modified). 4 new trust boundaries introduced (LLM-CLI, CLI-filesystem, CLI-git subprocess, auto-clear-claude). Data flows shift from in-memory prompt state to disk-persisted JSON sessions. Net architectural improvement: LLM control is bounded by CLI state machine. OWASP LLM01 surface reduced (smaller prompt) but new CLI input vector introduced.

4 trust boundaries assessed with 6 crossings analyzed. STRIDE analysis complete for 3 affected boundaries (LLM-CLI, CLI-filesystem, CLI-git). 13 new STRIDE threats identified, all in low-medium range. No high/critical threats. Key techniques: T1565.001 (stored data manipulation via session files), T1059.006 (CLI argument processing), T1068 (phase skipping via tampered state). Attack trees decomposed for governance review bypass. All threats are local-access-only.

3 attack paths validated: (1) Session state tampering to skip governance reviews — low likelihood, requires local filesystem access and schema knowledge; AuditLog provides partial detection. (2) Phase result injection via CLI — medium likelihood within designed trust model (LLM hallucination/prompt injection); downstream gh commands provide validation. (3) Auto-clear loop abuse — low likelihood, default 50-retry cap and exponential backoff mitigate. No unmitigated high-impact paths.

3 configuration findings: (1) Session directory created with default umask — low severity, recommend explicit mode=0o700. (2) Python version lowered to 3.9 — informational, code uses __future__ annotations for compatibility. (3) pytest-html report path in source tree — informational, should be in .gitignore. No IAM changes, no network exposure changes, no encryption changes.

4 techniques require detection coverage for PR changes. AuditLog JSONL provides medium-confidence detection for 2 techniques (T1565.001, T1059.006). 2 techniques have no detection (T1574.007 PATH interception, T1496 resource hijacking). 3 alerting gaps identified: no session file integrity verification, no CLI result schema validation, no cost monitoring on auto-clear. No new Sigma rules recommended — detections are best handled by AuditLog correlation.

6 ATT&CK techniques mapped to PR changes across 4 tactics (Execution, Impact, Privilege Escalation, Collection). All techniques have partial coverage via existing controls (argparse, AuditLog, backoff). Execution and Impact tactics have orange (partial) coverage. Collection has red (uncovered) — session files lack access controls. 2 Purple Team validation exercises planned for session tampering and false result injection.

3 vulnerabilities identified: VULN-01 (CVSS 3.3 Low) — no schema validation on --result CLI input; VULN-02 (CVSS 4.7 Medium) — session file integrity not verified, no HMAC/checksum; VULN-03 (CVSS 4.4 Medium) — no explicit file permissions on session directory/files. No critical or high vulnerabilities. All findings are local-access-only. Subprocess call to git is well-hardened (list-form, no interpolation, timeout).

4 regulatory controls assessed. SOC 2 CC6.1 (Logical Access) partially met — session persistence introduces new data store without explicit access controls. SOC 2 CC8.1 (Change Management) met — AuditLog provides change traceability. NIST SI-7 (Integrity) partially met — session files lack integrity verification. NIST AU-3 (Audit Records) met — comprehensive audit events with timestamps and session IDs. No data handling, PII, or GDPR impact.
